answer:
**Manusmṛti 12.56** states that a Brahmana who drinks liquor is reborn in lowly forms due to transmigration (saṃsāra), including as a **worm, insect, or moth**:

> A Brahmana who drinks liquor shall go to the state of **worms, insects, and moths**, and of birds that eat filth, and of violent creatures.[^1]

This verse falls in the chapter's section on the fruits of karma (12.3–81), which details how actions determine rebirths across species, tying into the broader discussion of the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their associated states (12.24–50). A related categorization appears earlier at 12.42, listing "**worms and insects**" among the lowest tāmasic rebirths (immovable things, worms and insects, fish, etc.).[^2]

No verse in Chapter 12 uses the exact phrasing "takes the form of," but 12.56 directly assigns these insect/worm forms to the soul (jīva) based on sin.

[^1]: Manusmṛti, [Chapter 12, paragraph for Mn_12.56](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-[id-for-12.56]).

For answer:**In Manusmṛti Chapter 12, verse 56 (Mn_12.56), a Brahmin who drinks liquor undergoes transmigration into the forms of worms, insects, and moths:**

> A Brahmana who drinks liquor shall go to the state of worms, insects, and moths, and of birds that eat filth, and of violent creatures.[^1]

This is part of the text's exposition on how karma determines rebirth (saṃsāra), specifically for grave sins like drinking liquor by a Brahmin.[^2] A nearby verse (12.42) also categorizes worms and insects as rebirth forms for those dominated by tamas.[^3]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, *Chapter 12*, [Mn_12.56](/works/unknown-manusmr
